behaviors.description: The traveling-salesman heuristic produces a route that is no more than 1.5x the length of the optimal route for inputs up to 20 stops
behaviors.justification: The failure is in the algorithm itself—the heuristic implementation. This is pure computation with no external dependencies. Generate synthetic test instances (5, 10, 20 stops with known coordinates), compute or precompute optimal solutions, run the heuristic, and verify the distance ratio is ≤1.5. No I/O, no subprocess, no external API calls needed.
behaviors.test_description: Create TSP instances with 5, 10, and 20 stops using synthetic coordinates. For each instance, compute the optimal route length (via precomputed benchmark, dynamic programming, or brute force for small sizes). Run the heuristic and measure the resulting route distance. Verify that heuristic_distance / optimal_distance ≤ 1.5 for all test instances.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test is purely computational with in-memory test data. No mocking of the heuristic—testing the real algorithm. Matches Unit level (single function boundary, no I/O).

behaviors.description: The delivery time window estimates are computed correctly from the distance between consecutive stops and the configured average speed for the road type
behaviors.justification: The behavior couples distance calculation with time window calculation. Failures could hide in the integration: wrong units (miles vs. km, seconds vs. hours), incorrect speed lookup, or formula misapplication. Testing distance in isolation (mocked) would miss real failures in the distance→speed→time pipeline. Need to wire distance calculation, speed lookup, and time computation together.
behaviors.test_description: Create a route with known consecutive stops (synthetic coordinates). Provide or mock the average speed for each road type. For each segment, verify: (1) distance is calculated from coordinates, (2) average speed is looked up for the road type, (3) time is computed as distance/speed in correct units (e.g., hours from km and km/h), (4) result matches expected values. Test with multiple segments and road types.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires multiple modules wired together (distance calculation, speed configuration lookup, time formula). Cannot mock distance and still catch real failures. Confirms Integration level.

behaviors.description: The shift-hours validator rejects a route whose total estimated time exceeds the driver's remaining shift hours, returning the excess amount
behaviors.justification: The validator is a single pure comparison function: if total_time > remaining_hours, reject and return excess. Failures would be in the comparison operator (>, >=) or in the arithmetic (subtraction). This is deterministic, testable with numeric inputs, no I/O, no external systems.
behaviors.test_description: Call the shift-hours validator with pre-calculated total times and remaining shift hours. Test cases: (1) total_time < remaining_hours → should accept route, (2) total_time == remaining_hours → should accept route, (3) total_time > remaining_hours → should reject route and return excess amount equal to (total_time - remaining_hours). Verify both the boolean accept/reject and the excess value are correct.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Pure function logic with numeric inputs and arithmetic output. No dependencies on other modules, no I/O. The test validates the validator's logic in isolation, which is appropriate at Unit level.

behaviors.description: When one address in the list fails geocoding (API returns no results), the service skips that stop and optimizes the remaining addresses, reporting which addresses were skipped
behaviors.justification: The behavior spans multiple modules: geocoding API call, error handling, address filtering, report generation, and optimization on the reduced set. A Unit test with a mocked geocoding function might pass even if error recovery, filtering, or reporting logic is broken. A real failure would involve the integration of these steps. Minimum is Integration: mock the geocoding API to return failure for one address, wire the components together, and verify the entire error-handling pipeline works.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the geocoding API to return success for N-1 addresses and no results for 1 address. Call the service with a list of N addresses. Verify: (1) the service skips the failed address and does not include it in optimization, (2) optimization proceeds correctly on the remaining N-1 addresses, (3) the service reports which address(es) were skipped and why (no geocoding results), (4) the returned route does not contain the skipped stop, (5) delivery items for the skipped address are handled correctly (dropped or reported).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires mocking the geocoding API and wiring error handling, filtering, reporting, and optimization together. Cannot test error handling in isolation without missing integration bugs. Confirms Integration level.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses in the input list are consolidated into a single stop with combined delivery items, not visited twice
behaviors.justification: Deduplication and consolidation is a single function processing a list: detect duplicate addresses (by string comparison), merge them into one stop, and combine delivery items. This is pure in-process logic with no I/O, no external systems. Failures would be in duplicate detection, item consolidation, or output structure—all testable at Unit level.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a list of addresses with 2-3 duplicates, each with distinct delivery items (e.g., quantities, item types). Call the deduplication and consolidation function. Verify: (1) each unique address appears exactly once in the output, (2) delivery items from duplicate entries are correctly combined (e.g., quantities summed or lists concatenated), (3) no address is visited twice, (4) address metadata (coordinates, type) is preserved correctly for consolidated stops.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test is pure list processing logic with in-memory data. No external dependencies, no I/O. Assumes duplicates are detected by address string comparison (not by geocoded coordinates). Matches Unit level.
